title: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome
chief_complaint: My patient with schizophrenia is rigid, confused, and has a fever of 104°F. He was started on haloperidol 2 weeks ago and the dose was increased 3 days ago. He's barely responsive.

physical_exam.General: Stuporous (GCS 10). Lead-pipe rigidity throughout all limbs.
physical_exam.Neurologic: Diffuse rigidity. BRADYreflexia (hyporeflexia). No clonus. Tremor present.
physical_exam.Skin: Dry, hot skin (NOT diaphoretic - different from serotonin syndrome).

hidden_data.cbc: WBC 18,500 with left shift, Hb 14.0, Platelets 210,000
hidden_data.cmp: Creatinine 2.2 (AKI from rhabdomyolysis), K+ 6.2, CK 15,000 (severe rhabdomyolysis), AST 250, ALT 200
hidden_data.medication: Haloperidol 15mg BID (increased from 5mg BID 3 days ago). Also on Lithium 900mg daily (level 1.2 - therapeutic).

correct_diagnosis: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS) from Haloperidol (High-Potency Typical Antipsychotic)
key_learning_points: NMS = life-threatening idiosyncratic reaction to dopamine antagonists (antipsychotics). Most common with high-potency typical antipsychotics (Haloperidol, Fluphenazine) but can occur with atypicals
Pathophysiology: Acute dopamine D2 receptor blockade in hypothalamus (hyperthermia), basal ganglia (rigidity), and autonomic pathways (dysautonomia)

Classic tetrad: Severe muscle rigidity (lead-pipe), Hyperthermia (>38°C, can exceed 41°C), Autonomic instability (tachycardia, labile BP, diaphoresis), Mental status changes (delirium → stupor → coma)

Lab: CK severely elevated (>1,000, often >10,000 from rhabdomyolysis), Leukocytosis, Elevated LFTs, Metabolic acidosis. Differentiate from serotonin syndrome: NMS = bradyreflexia, dry skin, constipation, gradual onset (days to weeks)

Treatment: STOP all dopamine antagonists. Supportive care: Cooling, IV fluids, benzodiazepines (for sedation and mild muscle relaxation). Dantrolene (muscle relaxant, inhibits Ca release from SR) 2-3mg/kg IV q6h. Bromocriptine (D2 agonist) 2.5-5mg PO/NG q6-8h. ICU admission. Amantadine alternative. ECT if refractory
Mortality 5-20% (usually from rhabdomyolysis, renal failure, cardiac arrhythmias, DIC). Rechallenge with antipsychotic after recovery (wait 2 weeks, start low-dose low-potency atypical)
